content_html:
July 19, 2004

Version 0.6 (Changes)

Proposed by Bryan Ford

Abstract

Many applications create and manage directories containing cached information about content stored elsewhere, such as cached Web content or thumbnail-size versions of images or movies. For speed and storage efficiency we would often like to avoid backing up, archiving, or otherwise unnecessarily copying such directories around, but it is a pain to identify and individually exclude each such directory during data transfer operations. I propose an extremely simple convention by which applications can reliably "tag" any cache directories they create, for easy identification by backup systems and other data management utilities. Data management utilities can then heed or ignore these tags as the user sees fit.

The Problem

Many application programs create and manage cache directories of some kind: directories in which they store temporary information whose storage can benefit the application's performance, but which can easily be regenerated from other "primary" data sources if it is lost. For example, most web browsers have a cache directory in which they store downloaded Internet content (Mozilla typically uses a directory called Cache somewhere under $(HOME)/.mozilla). When backing up, synchronizing, or transferring a user's home directory from one machine to another, we would usually like to avoid saving or transferring cache directories at all, because:

  • The contents of a cache directory generally has no long-term archival value, since it just mirrors source material stored elsewhere and often in a reduced or incomplete form - e.g., only the parts of a web page that the user has visited recently, or thumbnail-sized versions of pictures the user has browsed.
  • Cache directories can grow to a fairly substantial size - especially cache directories for web browsers - potentially wasting considerable backup storage space and/or network bandwidth.
  • The contents of a cache directory tends to change frequently even when the user is merely browsing or viewing files, which confuses incremental backup or synchronization software into seeing many frequent "false changes" in the directory tree and defeating the efficiency of the incremental algorithm.
  • The files in a cache directory are often named very cryptically (e.g., based on a hash of the name or content of the original source material). Seeing thousands of such meaningless filenames scroll past during operations that traverse a user's home directory is annoying at best, and may confuse or frighten inexperienced users.

There are a great many applications that use cache directories, however. While some evolving standards attempt to standardize the locations of such directories, most applications create their own cache directories in unpredictable locations and manage them independently of other applications. It therefore becomes very tedious, even for experienced users who can easily recognize cache directories, to figure out and list all of the appropriate --exclude options for backups or other directory traversal operations. Some applications, such as recent versions of KDE, keep their cache directories in /tmp or /var/tmp, which at least partly solves this problem by getting the cached content out of the user's home directory entirely. But there are good reasons an application or user might not want these caches in /tmp or /var/tmp: for example, those directories may be on a different partition with limited space, or files in them may be cleared out by the system too frequently (e.g., after a day, or on every reboot) to allow the cache to serve its intended purpose effectively.

The Filesystem Hierarchy Standard specifies a system-wide /var/cache directory intended to hold such cached information, but this directory is not generally world-writable, and thus can only be used by applications pre-packaged with the system or installed by the system administrator. The XDG Base Directory Specification instead recommends that applications create their caches in a location specified by a user environment variable, or in a standard subdirectory of the user's home directory if that environment variable doesn't exist. The XDG convention allows applications to operate without special permissions, but it does not help systemwide backup or data management utilities locate individual users' cache directories reliably, because each user might set the environment variable differently in his or her login profile.

Proposed Solution

Given the present non-existence of any perfect agreement on where applications should store their cached information, I propose a very simple convention that will at least allow such information to be identified effectively. Regardless of where the application decides to (or is configured to) place its cache directory, it should place within this directory a file named: 

CACHEDIR.TAG

This file must be an ordinary file, not for example a symbolic link. Additionally, the first 43 octets of this file must consist of the following ASCII header string: 

Signature: 8a477f597d28d172789f06886806bc55

Case is important in the header signature, there can be no whitespace or other characters in the file before the 'S', and there is exactly one space character (ASCII code 32 decimal) after the colon. The header string does not have to be followed by an LF or CR/LF in the file in order for the file to be recognized as a valid cache directory tag. The hex value in the signature happens to be the MD5 hash of the string ".IsCacheDirectory". This signature header is required to avoid the chance of an unrelated file named CACHEDIR.TAG being mistakenly interpreted as a cache directory tag by data management utilities, and (for example) causing valuable data not to be backed up.

The content of the remainder of the tag file is currently unspecified, except that it should be a plain text file in UTF-8 encoding, and any line beginning with a hash character ('#') should be treated as a comment and ignored by any software that reads the file.

We will henceforth refer to a file named as specified above, and having the required signature at the beginning of its content, as a cache directory tag.

For the benefit of anyone who happens to find and look at a cache directory tag directly, it is recommended that applications include in the file a comment referring back to this specification. For example: 

Signature: 8a477f597d28d172789f06886806bc55
# This file is a cache directory tag created by (application name).
# For information about cache directory tags, see:
#	http://www.brynosaurus.com/cachedir/

The official "home" URL of this specification may change from the above at some point if/when this proposal becomes more of a formal standard, but in that case an appropriate forwarding link will be left in the old location.

Application Semantics

The presence of a cache directory tag within a given directory indicates that the entire contents of that directory, including any and all subdirectories underneath it, consists of cached information that can be re-generated if necessary from appropriate source material located elsewhere. An application that creates and/or uses a cache directory should write a single cache directory tag into the topmost directory whose contents represent cached information. For example, if Mozilla creates a directory named $(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache to hold cached content downloaded from the Internet, it would then create within it a cache directory tag named: $(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache/CACHEDIR.TAG. Only one cache directory tag is required to tag an entire subdirectory tree of cached content. The application should also regenerate the cache directory tag if it disappears: e.g., if the entire contents of the cache directory is deleted without the directory itself being deleted.

Data backup, transfer, or synchronization software, if configured to do so, may interpret the presence of a cache directory tag in a directory as a command to exclude that directory automatically (including any subdirectories) from the backup or transfer operation. I leave it to the designers of such applications to decide whether this "auto-exclude" behavior should be the default or only adopted when specifically requested by the user. A bit of conservatism is probably warranted in the short term, but automatically excluding cache directories by default could improve efficiency and ease-of-use in the longer term.

For maximum robustness against accidental data loss, any tool that has built-in support for cache directory tags is strongly encouraged to verify that any file it finds named CACHEDIR.TAG is a regular file and actually contains the 43-byte header specified above, before concluding that the file is indeed a cache directory tag.

User-friendly file managers and other directory hierarchy browsing software may also want to notice the presence of a cache directory tag in a directory, and inform the user that the contents of the directory is automatically managed and is not likely to make much sense if "browsed" directly.

System administration software such as du might provide a way to locate, total, and report the space usage of all the cache directories on a partition, helping the administrator to determine how much "soft" space is in use on a file system that could be easily freed up if necessary. Similarly, a utility might allow the administrator to clean out all the cache directories on the system, or to delete files in cache directories beyond a certain "age."

System software should not, however, just periodically go through and unilaterally delete "old" files in arbitrary cache directories on a purely automatic basis. Different applications need to be able to manage their caches differently; cached data that is considered "old" to one application or to the system might not be considered "old" to another (e.g., if certain data takes a long time to regenerate). In the future this standard might be extended to allow applications to write additional meta-data in their cache directory tags, providing system utilities with the additional information they would need to manage application caches intelligently on a global basis. For now, however, the primary purpose for introducing cache directory tags is merely to make it easy to avoid backing up or copying cached data unnecessarily from one machine to another.

Security Considerations

"Blind" use of cache directory tags in automatic system backups could potentially increase the damage that intruders or malware could cause to a system. A user or system administrator might be substantially less likely to notice the malicious insertion of a

CACHDIR.TAG

into an important directory than the outright deletion of that directory, for example, causing the contents of that directory to be omitted from regular backups. To mitigate this risk, backup software should at least inform the user which directories are being omitted due to the presence of cache directory tags. Automatic incremental backup software might maintain a list of "approved" cache directories, and whenever new cache directory tags appear, only heed them after being approved by the system administrator. In short, to maintain robustness of backups in the face of security compromises, cache directory tags should only be treated as hints, never as "the final word" on what should and should not be backed up.

Alternative Approaches

This section discusses a few of the alternatives that were considered in developing this proposal, and the rationale for designing the proposal as specified. It is formatted as a mini-FAQ addressing the most common questions.

  • Why not just standardize the location where applications keep their cache directories? First, there are already multiple conflicting standards: e.g., the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard specifies /var/cache, KDE currently uses /var/tmp, and the XDG Base Directory Specification specifies the use of a user-customizable environment variable, falling back on $(HOME)/.cache in the user's home directory. Even if one such standard eventually "wins", there is inevitable tension between standardizing the placement of application caches and allowing users to customize it. The XDG specification clearly recognizes this tension and accounts for it, but its very flexibility makes it difficult or impossible for systemwide utilities to locate each user's cache directory reliably, even if all applications were to conform to the XDG standard.

  • Why not just use the existing "no-backup" or "no-dump" extended file attributes that some operating systems support? Because only some operating systems (and generally only certain specific file systems in them) support those attributes. Any proposed convention for tagging cache directories that depends on non-portable OS features would inherently have limited applicability and would not likely be widely adopted among portable applications. Applications are free to support system-specific extensions when available, but for maximum usefulness we really need one extremely simple, portable convention that caching applications can adopt with almost no effort, and that is the purpose of this proposal.

  • Why shouldn't all web browsers just use the same cache? Sharing a single Internet content cache among multiple web browsers or other web applications, all of which might be running concurrently, is a very useful and important goal - but it presents considerable technical challenges that are still being worked out. Even when such sharing is achieved for web content caches, however, many other applications maintain other types of on-disk caches in which they store entirely different kinds of data, such as intermediate results for long-running computations or swap space for large data sets. It is unreasonable to expect applications to share caches when those caches serve entirely different purposes and inherently must be managed differently. But it would still be useful, and it is much more realistic, to expect applications at least to make their caches easily identifiable as such by adopting a simple convention such as the one proposed here.

  • Why does the proposal only support cache directories - what if an application only has a single file that it uses as a cache? Because the tagging scheme is much simpler and easier to take advantage of this way, and imposes no great burden on caching applications. If you're not paranoid about checking cache directory tag file headers, then most existing, unmodified backup/archive applications can immediately take advantage of this tagging convention via some pretty trivial shell-fu. For example: 

    	find . -name CACHEDIR.TAG | sed -e 's/[/]CACHEDIR.TAG$//g' >/tmp/excludes
	tar cvf /tmp/backup.tar --exclude-from=/tmp/excludes .
    Trying to support the tagging of individual files within a directory would probably mean at least having to parse text files and scan them for file names, making the convention much more complex and potentially delicate at the outset. And when it comes to standards that affect what data gets backed up and what doesn't, we obviously would like them to be as simple and robust as possible.

    For those existing caching applications that keep all their cached data in a single file, it should not be inordinately difficult for them just to move that cache file down one level into its own separate subdirectory, and write a cache directory tag into that new subdirectory alongside the cache file. Such a slight directory structure change should not cause any backward compatibility problems, since by definition any cache file in the old location is regenerable and can just be deleted or moved. And users won't care because they aren't normally expected to see directly or understand the application's cache files or directories anyway.

Conclusion

By adopting the convention described above of writing cache directory tags where appropriate, applications that create and manage cache directories will make it much easier for users and system administrators to identify and manage "soft" storage areas whose contents are useful for performance but do not need to be backed up or synchronized across machines. This proposal deliberately attempts to adopt the simplest reasonable and portable solution, in the interest of maximizing robustness while minimizing cost of adoption, and hence hopefully maximizing its chance of eventual widespread adoption. The proposal nevertheless leaves room for future enhancements, in which applications might include within their cache directory tags additional useful information about the semantics and management of their cache directories.

Changes

  • Version 0.6, November 4, 2019: Web page consolidation and restructuring; no substantive changes.

  • Version 0.5, October 27, 2004: Added Security Considerations section in response to a potential vulnerability pointed out by Gregory P. Smith.

  • Version 0.4, August 10, 2004: Changed cache tag file name from ".IsCacheDirectory" to "CACHEDIR.TAG", to be compatible with old 14-character POSIX file systems and 8.3-character MS-DOS file systems.

  • Version 0.3, July 29, 2004: Recommend that apps include a comment in their tags referring back to this spec. Recommend that backup/archival apps verify tag headers. Discuss tag filename length issue.

  • Version 0.2, July 23, 2004: Fleshed out Alternative Approaches section based on various feedback. Removed reference to thumbnail managing standard since the thumbnails directory doesn't quite have pure cache semantics.

  • Version 0.1, July 19, 2004: First public release.

Application Support Status

Caching Applications Archive/Backup Tools
The following applications are known to create and maintain caches of some kind in the user's home directory.

Applications that currently support this specification:

  • BZFlag multi-player 3D game as of 2004-07-24. (Feature Request))
  • libdvdcss as of 2004-08-12. (Feature Request)
  • VLC as of 2004-10-27.
  • WWWOFFLE as of 2006-02-05.

Positive responses received from developers of:

  • Mozilla web browser (Feature Request)
  • Galeon web browser
  • OpenOffice.org (Feature Request)
  • ccache

Discussion initiated with developers of:

  • The GIMP
  • aRts/MCOP
The following archival tools support this tagging convention:
  • tar archiving/backup tool:
    use the new --exclude-caches option.
    (patch to tar-1.14) (patch to tar-CVS)

Other archival tools that are not (yet) directly sensitive to cache tags, but can be made so through the use of appropriate options or shell magic:

  • cpio archiving/backup tool
  • rsync incremental transfer/synchronization tool
  • DAR disk archiver (Feature Request)

Please let me know of other applications you know of that should be on this list!

Related Links

  • Filesystem Hierarchy Standard
  • XDG Base Directory Specification
  • Thumbnail Managing Standard

blocks.html_en: Many applications create and manage directories containing cached information about content stored elsewhere, such as cached Web content or thumbnail-size versions of images or movies. For speed and storage efficiency we would often like to avoid backing up, archiving, or otherwise unnecessarily copying such directories around, but it is a pain to identify and individually exclude each such directory during data transfer operations. I propose an extremely simple convention by which applications can reliably "tag" any cache directories they create, for easy identification by backup systems and other data management utilities. Data management utilities can then heed or ignore these tags as the user sees fit.

sentences.en: Many applications create and manage directories containing cached information about content stored elsewhere, such as cached Web content or thumbnail-size versions of images or movies.
sentences.zh: 许多应用程序创建和管理目录，其中包含有关存储在其他位置的内容的缓存信息，例如缓存的 Web 内容或图像或电影的缩略图大小版本。

sentences.en: For speed and storage efficiency we would often like to avoid backing up, archiving, or otherwise unnecessarily copying such directories around, but it is a pain to identify and individually exclude each such directory during data transfer operations.
sentences.zh: 为了速度和存储效率，我们通常希望避免备份、归档或以其他方式不必要地复制此类目录，但在数据传输操作期间识别并单独排除每个此类目录是一件痛苦的事情。

sentences.en: I propose an extremely simple convention by which applications can reliably "tag" any cache directories they create, for easy identification by backup systems and other data management utilities.
sentences.zh: 我提出了一种极其简单的约定，应用程序可以通过该约定可靠地“标记”它们创建的任何缓存目录，以便备份系统和其他数据管理实用程序轻松识别。

sentences.en: Data management utilities can then heed or ignore these tags as the user sees fit.
sentences.zh: 然后，数据管理实用程序可以根据用户认为合适的情况留意或忽略这些标签。

blocks.html_en: The Problem

sentences.en: The Problem
sentences.zh: 问题

blocks.html_en: Many application programs create and manage cache directories of some kind: directories in which they store temporary information whose storage can benefit the application's performance, but which can easily be regenerated from other "primary" data sources if it is lost. For example, most web browsers have a cache directory in which they store downloaded Internet content (Mozilla typically uses a directory called Cache somewhere under $(HOME)/.mozilla). When backing up, synchronizing, or transferring a user's home directory from one machine to another, we would usually like to avoid saving or transferring cache directories at all, because:

sentences.en: Many application programs create and manage cache directories of some kind: directories in which they store temporary information whose storage can benefit the application's performance, but which can easily be regenerated from other "primary" data sources if it is lost.
sentences.zh: 许多应用程序创建和管理某种类型的缓存目录：在其中存储临时信息的目录，这些信息的存储可以有利于应用程序的性能，但如果丢失，可以轻松地从其他“主”数据源重新生成。

sentences.en: For example, most web browsers have a cache directory in which they store downloaded Internet content (Mozilla typically uses a directory called Cache somewhere under $(HOME)/.mozilla).
sentences.zh: 例如，大多数 Web 浏览器都有一个缓存目录，用于存储下载的 Internet 内容（Mozilla 通常在 $(HOME)/.mozilla 下的某处使用名为 Cache 的目录）。

sentences.en: When backing up, synchronizing, or transferring a user's home directory from one machine to another, we would usually like to avoid saving or transferring cache directories at all, because:
sentences.zh: 当备份、同步或将用户的主目录从一台计算机传输到另一台计算机时，我们通常希望完全避免保存或传输缓存目录，因为：

blocks.html_en: • The contents of a cache directory generally has no long-term archival value, since it just mirrors source material stored elsewhere and often in a reduced or incomplete form - e.g., only the parts of a web page that the user has visited recently, or thumbnail-sized versions of pictures the user has browsed.

sentences.en: The contents of a cache directory generally has no long-term archival value, since it just mirrors source material stored elsewhere and often in a reduced or incomplete form - e.g., only the parts of a web page that the user has visited recently, or thumbnail-sized versions of pictures the user has browsed.
sentences.zh: 缓存目录的内容通常没有长期存档价值，因为它仅反映存储在其他地方的源材料，并且通常以缩减或不完整的形式 - 例如，仅反映用户最近访问过的网页部分，或者用户浏览过的图片的缩略图大小版本。

blocks.html_en: • Cache directories can grow to a fairly substantial size - especially cache directories for web browsers - potentially wasting considerable backup storage space and/or network bandwidth.

sentences.en: Cache directories can grow to a fairly substantial size - especially cache directories for web browsers - potentially wasting considerable backup storage space and/or network bandwidth.
sentences.zh: 缓存目录可能会增长到相当大的大小 - 特别是 Web 浏览器的缓存目录 - 可能会浪费大量的备份存储空间和/或网络带宽。

blocks.html_en: • The contents of a cache directory tends to change frequently even when the user is merely browsing or viewing files, which confuses incremental backup or synchronization software into seeing many frequent "false changes" in the directory tree and defeating the efficiency of the incremental algorithm.

sentences.en: The contents of a cache directory tends to change frequently even when the user is merely browsing or viewing files, which confuses incremental backup or synchronization software into seeing many frequent "false changes" in the directory tree and defeating the efficiency of the incremental algorithm.
sentences.zh: 即使用户只是浏览或查看文件，缓存目录的内容也往往会频繁更改，这会混淆增量备份或同步软件，使其在目录树中看到许多频繁的“错误更改”，从而降低增量算法的效率。

blocks.html_en: • The files in a cache directory are often named very cryptically (e.g., based on a hash of the name or content of the original source material). Seeing thousands of such meaningless filenames scroll past during operations that traverse a user's home directory is annoying at best, and may confuse or frighten inexperienced users.

sentences.en: The files in a cache directory are often named very cryptically (e.g., based on a hash of the name or content of the original source material).
sentences.zh: 缓存目录中的文件通常命名非常神秘（例如，基于原始源材料的名称或内容的哈希）。

sentences.en: Seeing thousands of such meaningless filenames scroll past during operations that traverse a user's home directory is annoying at best, and may confuse or frighten inexperienced users.
sentences.zh: 在遍历用户主目录的操作过程中，看到数千个此类无意义的文件名滚动过去，这充其量是令人烦恼的，并且可能会令没有经验的用户感到困惑或害怕。

blocks.html_en: There are a great many applications that use cache directories, however. While some evolving standards attempt to standardize the locations of such directories, most applications create their own cache directories in unpredictable locations and manage them independently of other applications. It therefore becomes very tedious, even for experienced users who can easily recognize cache directories, to figure out and list all of the appropriate --exclude options for backups or other directory traversal operations. Some applications, such as recent versions of KDE, keep their cache directories in /tmp or /var/tmp, which at least partly solves this problem by getting the cached content out of the user's home directory entirely. But there are good reasons an application or user might not want these caches in /tmp or /var/tmp: for example, those directories may be on a different partition with limited space, or files in them may be cleared out by the system too frequently (e.g., after a day, or on every reboot) to allow the cache to serve its intended purpose effectively.

sentences.en: There are a great many applications that use cache directories, however.
sentences.zh: 然而，有很多应用程序使用缓存目录。

sentences.en: While some evolving standards attempt to standardize the locations of such directories, most applications create their own cache directories in unpredictable locations and manage them independently of other applications.
sentences.zh: 虽然一些不断发展的标准试图标准化此类目录的位置，但大多数应用程序在不可预测的位置创建自己的缓存目录，并独立于其他应用程序来管理它们。

sentences.en: It therefore becomes very tedious, even for experienced users who can easily recognize cache directories, to figure out and list all of the appropriate --exclude options for backups or other directory traversal operations.
sentences.zh: 因此，即使对于能够轻松识别缓存目录的经验丰富的用户来说，找出并列出用于备份或其他目录遍历操作的所有适当的 --exclude 选项也变得非常乏味。

sentences.en: Some applications, such as recent versions of KDE, keep their cache directories in /tmp or /var/tmp, which at least partly solves this problem by getting the cached content out of the user's home directory entirely.
sentences.zh: 某些应用程序（例如最新版本的 KDE）将其缓存目录保留在 /tmp 或 /var/tmp 中，这通过将缓存内容完全从用户的主目录中获取来至少部分解决了这个问题。

sentences.en: But there are good reasons an application or user might not want these caches in /tmp or /var/tmp: for example, those directories may be on a different partition with limited space, or files in them may be cleared out by the system too frequently (e.g., after a day, or on every reboot) to allow the cache to serve its intended purpose effectively.
sentences.zh: 但是，应用程序或用户可能不希望将这些缓存放在 /tmp 或 /var/tmp 中：例如，这些目录可能位于空间有限的不同分区上，或者其中的文件可能被系统过于频繁地清除（例如，一天后或每次重新启动时），以允许缓存有效地达到其预期目的。

blocks.html_en: The Filesystem Hierarchy Standard specifies a system-wide /var/cache directory intended to hold such cached information, but this directory is not generally world-writable, and thus can only be used by applications pre-packaged with the system or installed by the system administrator. The XDG Base Directory Specification instead recommends that applications create their caches in a location specified by a user environment variable, or in a standard subdirectory of the user's home directory if that environment variable doesn't exist. The XDG convention allows applications to operate without special permissions, but it does not help systemwide backup or data management utilities locate individual users' cache directories reliably, because each user might set the environment variable differently in his or her login profile.

sentences.en: The Filesystem Hierarchy Standard specifies a system-wide /var/cache directory intended to hold such cached information, but this directory is not generally world-writable, and thus can only be used by applications pre-packaged with the system or installed by the system administrator.
sentences.zh: 文件系统层次结构标准指定了一个系统范围的 /var/cache 目录，旨在保存此类缓存信息，但该目录通常不是全局可写的，因此只能由系统预打包或由系统管理员安装的应用程序使用。

sentences.en: The XDG Base Directory Specification instead recommends that applications create their caches in a location specified by a user environment variable, or in a standard subdirectory of the user's home directory if that environment variable doesn't exist.
sentences.zh: XDG 基本目录规范建议应用程序在用户环境变量指定的位置创建缓存，如果该环境变量不存在，则在用户主目录的标准子目录中创建缓存。

sentences.en: The XDG convention allows applications to operate without special permissions, but it does not help systemwide backup or data management utilities locate individual users' cache directories reliably, because each user might set the environment variable differently in his or her login profile.
sentences.zh: XDG 约定允许应用程序在没有特殊权限的情况下运行，但它不能帮助系统范围的备份或数据管理实用程序可靠地定位各个用户的缓存目录，因为每个用户可能在其登录配置文件中设置不同的环境变量。

blocks.html_en: Proposed Solution

sentences.en: Proposed Solution
sentences.zh: 建议的解决方案

blocks.html_en: Given the present non-existence of any perfect agreement on where applications should store their cached information, I propose a very simple convention that will at least allow such information to be identified effectively. Regardless of where the application decides to (or is configured to) place its cache directory, it should place within this directory a file named:

sentences.en: Given the present non-existence of any perfect agreement on where applications should store their cached information, I propose a very simple convention that will at least allow such information to be identified effectively.
sentences.zh: 鉴于目前对于应用程序应在何处存储其缓存信息不存在任何完美的协议，我提出了一个非常简单的约定，至少允许有效地识别此类信息。

sentences.en: Regardless of where the application decides to (or is configured to) place its cache directory, it should place within this directory a file named:
sentences.zh: 无论应用程序决定（或配置为）将其缓存目录放置在何处，它都应该在此目录中放置一个名为：

blocks.html_en: CACHEDIR.TAG

sentences.en: CACHEDIR.TAG
sentences.zh: 缓存目录标签

blocks.html_en: This file must be an ordinary file, not for example a symbolic link. Additionally, the first 43 octets of this file must consist of the following ASCII header string:

sentences.en: This file must be an ordinary file, not for example a symbolic link.
sentences.zh: 该文件必须是普通文件，例如不能是符号链接。

sentences.en: Additionally, the first 43 octets of this file must consist of the following ASCII header string:
sentences.zh: 此外，该文件的前 43 个八位字节必须包含以下 ASCII 标头字符串：

blocks.html_en: Signature: 8a477f597d28d172789f06886806bc55

Case is important in the header signature, there can be no whitespace or other characters in the file before the 'S', and there is exactly one space character (ASCII code 32 decimal) after the colon. The header string does not have to be followed by an LF or CR/LF in the file in order for the file to be recognized as a valid cache directory tag. The hex value in the signature happens to be the MD5 hash of the string ".IsCacheDirectory". This signature header is required to avoid the chance of an unrelated file named CACHEDIR.TAG being mistakenly interpreted as a cache directory tag by data management utilities, and (for example) causing valuable data not to be backed up.

sentences.en: Case is important in the header signature, there can be no whitespace or other characters in the file before the 'S', and there is exactly one space character (ASCII code 32 decimal) after the colon.
sentences.zh: 标头签名中大小写很重要，文件中“S”之前不能有空格或其他字符，并且冒号之后恰好有一个空格字符（ASCII 代码 32 十进制）。

sentences.en: The header string does not have to be followed by an LF or CR/LF in the file in order for the file to be recognized as a valid cache directory tag.
sentences.zh: 文件中的标头字符串不必后跟 LF 或 CR/LF 才能将文件识别为有效的缓存目录标记。

sentences.en: The hex value in the signature happens to be the MD5 hash of the string ".IsCacheDirectory".
sentences.zh: 签名中的十六进制值恰好是字符串“.IsCacheDirectory”的 MD5 哈希值。

sentences.en: This signature header is required to avoid the chance of an unrelated file named CACHEDIR.TAG being mistakenly interpreted as a cache directory tag by data management utilities, and (for example) causing valuable data not to be backed up.
sentences.zh: 需要此签名标头以避免名为 CACHEDIR.TAG 的不相关文件被数据管理实用程序错误地解释为缓存目录标记，并且（例如）导致不备份有价值的数据。

blocks.html_en: The content of the remainder of the tag file is currently unspecified, except that it should be a plain text file in UTF-8 encoding, and any line beginning with a hash character ('#') should be treated as a comment and ignored by any software that reads the file.

sentences.en: The content of the remainder of the tag file is currently unspecified, except that it should be a plain text file in UTF-8 encoding, and any line beginning with a hash character ('#') should be treated as a comment and ignored by any software that reads the file.
sentences.zh: 标记文件的其余部分的内容当前未指定，但它应该是 UTF-8 编码的纯文本文件，并且任何以哈希字符（“#”）开头的行都应被视为注释，并被读取该文件的任何软件忽略。

blocks.html_en: We will henceforth refer to a file named as specified above, and having the required signature at the beginning of its content, as a cache directory tag.

sentences.en: We will henceforth refer to a file named as specified above, and having the required signature at the beginning of its content, as a cache directory tag.
sentences.zh: 此后，我们将引用上面指定的名称的文件，并在其内容的开头具有所需的签名，作为缓存目录标记。

blocks.html_en: For the benefit of anyone who happens to find and look at a cache directory tag directly, it is recommended that applications include in the file a comment referring back to this specification. For example:

sentences.en: For the benefit of anyone who happens to find and look at a cache directory tag directly, it is recommended that applications include in the file a comment referring back to this specification.
sentences.zh: 为了让任何偶然发现并直接查看缓存目录标记的人受益，建议应用程序在文件中包含引用此规范的注释。

sentences.en: For example:
sentences.zh: 例如：

blocks.html_en:
Signature: 8a477f597d28d172789f06886806bc55
# This file is a cache directory tag created by (application name).
# For information about cache directory tags, see:
#	http://www.brynosaurus.com/cachedir/

sentences.en: Signature: 8a477f597d28d172789f06886806bc55 # This file is a cache directory tag created by (application name). # For information about cache directory tags, see: # http://www.brynosaurus.com/cachedir/
sentences.zh: Signature: 8a477f597d28d172789f06886806bc55 # 该文件是（应用程序名称）创建的缓存目录标记。 # 有关缓存目录标签的信息，请参阅： # http://www.brynosaurus.com/cachedir/

blocks.html_en: The official "home" URL of this specification may change from the above at some point if/when this proposal becomes more of a formal standard, but in that case an appropriate forwarding link will be left in the old location.

sentences.en: The official "home" URL of this specification may change from the above at some point if/when this proposal becomes more of a formal standard, but in that case an appropriate forwarding link will be left in the old location.
sentences.zh: 如果/当本提案成为正式标准时，本规范的官方“主页”URL 可能会在某个时候从上述内容发生变化，但在这种情况下，适当的转发链接将保留在旧位置。

blocks.html_en: Application Semantics

sentences.en: Application Semantics
sentences.zh: 应用语义

blocks.html_en: The presence of a cache directory tag within a given directory indicates that the entire contents of that directory, including any and all subdirectories underneath it, consists of cached information that can be re-generated if necessary from appropriate source material located elsewhere. An application that creates and/or uses a cache directory should write a single cache directory tag into the topmost directory whose contents represent cached information. For example, if Mozilla creates a directory named $(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache to hold cached content downloaded from the Internet, it would then create within it a cache directory tag named: $(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache/CACHEDIR.TAG. Only one cache directory tag is required to tag an entire subdirectory tree of cached content. The application should also regenerate the cache directory tag if it disappears: e.g., if the entire contents of the cache directory is deleted without the directory itself being deleted.

sentences.en: The presence of a cache directory tag within a given directory indicates that the entire contents of that directory, including any and all subdirectories underneath it, consists of cached information that can be re-generated if necessary from appropriate source material located elsewhere.
sentences.zh: 给定目录中缓存目录标签的存在表明该目录的全部内容（包括其下的任何和所有子目录）由缓存信息组成，如果需要，可以从位于其他位置的适当源材料重新生成这些信息。

sentences.en: An application that creates and/or uses a cache directory should write a single cache directory tag into the topmost directory whose contents represent cached information.
sentences.zh: 创建和/或使用缓存目录的应用程序应将单个缓存目录标记写入其内容代表缓存信息的最顶层目录。

sentences.en: For example, if Mozilla creates a directory named $(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache to hold cached content downloaded from the Internet, it would then create within it a cache directory tag named: $(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache/CACHEDIR.TAG.
sentences.zh: 例如，如果 Mozilla 创建一个名为 $(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache 的目录来保存从 Internet 下载的缓存内容，那么它会在其中创建一个名为：$(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache/CACHEDIR.TAG 的缓存目录标记。

sentences.en: Only one cache directory tag is required to tag an entire subdirectory tree of cached content.
sentences.zh: 只需一个缓存目录标签即可标记缓存内容的整个子目录树。

sentences.en: The application should also regenerate the cache directory tag if it disappears: e.g., if the entire contents of the cache directory is deleted without the directory itself being deleted.
sentences.zh: 如果缓存目录标签消失，应用程序还应该重新生成缓存目录标签：例如，如果缓存目录的全部内容被删除而目录本身没有被删除。

blocks.html_en: Data backup, transfer, or synchronization software, if configured to do so, may interpret the presence of a cache directory tag in a directory as a command to exclude that directory automatically (including any subdirectories) from the backup or transfer operation. I leave it to the designers of such applications to decide whether this "auto-exclude" behavior should be the default or only adopted when specifically requested by the user. A bit of conservatism is probably warranted in the short term, but automatically excluding cache directories by default could improve efficiency and ease-of-use in the longer term.

sentences.en: Data backup, transfer, or synchronization software, if configured to do so, may interpret the presence of a cache directory tag in a directory as a command to exclude that directory automatically (including any subdirectories) from the backup or transfer operation.
sentences.zh: 数据备份、传输或同步软件（如果配置为这样做）可以将目录中存在的缓存目录标记解释为从备份或传输操作中自动排除该目录（包括任何子目录）的命令。

sentences.en: I leave it to the designers of such applications to decide whether this "auto-exclude" behavior should be the default or only adopted when specifically requested by the user.
sentences.zh: 我让此类应用程序的设计者来决定这种“自动排除”行为是否应该是默认行为，或者仅在用户明确要求时才采用。

sentences.en: A bit of conservatism is probably warranted in the short term, but automatically excluding cache directories by default could improve efficiency and ease-of-use in the longer term.
sentences.zh: 短期内可能需要采取一些保守措施，但从长远来看，默认情况下自动排除缓存目录可以提高效率和易用性。

blocks.html_en: For maximum robustness against accidental data loss, any tool that has built-in support for cache directory tags is strongly encouraged to verify that any file it finds named CACHEDIR.TAG is a regular file and actually contains the 43-byte header specified above, before concluding that the file is indeed a cache directory tag.

sentences.en: For maximum robustness against accidental data loss, any tool that has built-in support for cache directory tags is strongly encouraged to verify that any file it finds named CACHEDIR.TAG is a regular file and actually contains the 43-byte header specified above, before concluding that the file is indeed a cache directory tag.
sentences.zh: 为了最大程度地防止意外数据丢失，强烈建议任何具有缓存目录标记内置支持的工具在断定该文件确实是缓存目录标记之前验证它找到的名为 CACHEDIR.TAG 的任何文件是否是常规文件并且实际上包含上面指定的 43 字节标头。

blocks.html_en: User-friendly file managers and other directory hierarchy browsing software may also want to notice the presence of a cache directory tag in a directory, and inform the user that the contents of the directory is automatically managed and is not likely to make much sense if "browsed" directly.

sentences.en: User-friendly file managers and other directory hierarchy browsing software may also want to notice the presence of a cache directory tag in a directory, and inform the user that the contents of the directory is automatically managed and is not likely to make much sense if "browsed" directly.
sentences.zh: 用户友好的文件管理器和其他目录层次结构浏览软件可能还希望注意到目录中缓存目录标签的存在，并通知用户该目录的内容是自动管理的，如果直接“浏览”则不太可能有多大意义。

blocks.html_en: System administration software such as du might provide a way to locate, total, and report the space usage of all the cache directories on a partition, helping the administrator to determine how much "soft" space is in use on a file system that could be easily freed up if necessary. Similarly, a utility might allow the administrator to clean out all the cache directories on the system, or to delete files in cache directories beyond a certain "age."

sentences.en: System administration software such as du might provide a way to locate, total, and report the space usage of all the cache directories on a partition, helping the administrator to determine how much "soft" space is in use on a file system that could be easily freed up if necessary.
sentences.zh: 系统管理软件（例如 du）可能提供一种方法来定位、汇总和报告分区上所有缓存目录的空间使用情况，帮助管理员确定文件系统上正在使用多少“软”空间，必要时可以轻松释放这些空间。

sentences.en: Similarly, a utility might allow the administrator to clean out all the cache directories on the system, or to delete files in cache directories beyond a certain "age."
sentences.zh: 类似地，实用程序可能允许管理员清除系统上的所有缓存目录，或者删除缓存目录中超过特定​​“期限”的文件。

blocks.html_en: System software should not, however, just periodically go through and unilaterally delete "old" files in arbitrary cache directories on a purely automatic basis. Different applications need to be able to manage their caches differently; cached data that is considered "old" to one application or to the system might not be considered "old" to another (e.g., if certain data takes a long time to regenerate). In the future this standard might be extended to allow applications to write additional meta-data in their cache directory tags, providing system utilities with the additional information they would need to manage application caches intelligently on a global basis. For now, however, the primary purpose for introducing cache directory tags is merely to make it easy to avoid backing up or copying cached data unnecessarily from one machine to another.

sentences.en: Security Considerations
sentences.zh: 安全考虑

blocks.html_en: "Blind" use of cache directory tags in automatic system backups could potentially increase the damage that intruders or malware could cause to a system. A user or system administrator might be substantially less likely to notice the malicious insertion of a

blocks.html_en: into an important directory than the outright deletion of that directory, for example, causing the contents of that directory to be omitted from regular backups. To mitigate this risk, backup software should at least inform the user which directories are being omitted due to the presence of cache directory tags. Automatic incremental backup software might maintain a list of "approved" cache directories, and whenever new cache directory tags appear, only heed them after being approved by the system administrator. In short, to maintain robustness of backups in the face of security compromises, cache directory tags should only be treated as hints, never as "the final word" on what should and should not be backed up.

blocks.html_en: Alternative Approaches

sentences.en: Alternative Approaches
sentences.zh: 替代方法

blocks.html_en: This section discusses a few of the alternatives that were considered in developing this proposal, and the rationale for designing the proposal as specified. It is formatted as a mini-FAQ addressing the most common questions.

sentences.en: This section discusses a few of the alternatives that were considered in developing this proposal, and the rationale for designing the proposal as specified.
sentences.zh: 本节讨论在制定本提案时考虑的一些替代方案，以及按规定设计该提案的基本原理。

sentences.en: It is formatted as a mini-FAQ addressing the most common questions.
sentences.zh: 它采用迷你常见问题解答的形式，解决最常见的问题。

blocks.html_en:
• Why not just standardize the location where applications keep their cache directories? First, there are already multiple conflicting standards: e.g., the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard specifies /var/cache, KDE currently uses /var/tmp, and the XDG Base Directory Specification specifies the use of a user-customizable environment variable, falling back on $(HOME)/.cache in the user's home directory. Even if one such standard eventually "wins", there is inevitable tension between standardizing the placement of application caches and allowing users to customize it. The XDG specification clearly recognizes this tension and accounts for it, but its very flexibility makes it difficult or impossible for systemwide utilities to locate each user's cache directory reliably, even if all applications were to conform to the XDG standard.

  • Why not just use the existing "no-backup" or "no-dump" extended file attributes that some operating systems support? Because only some operating systems (and generally only certain specific file systems in them) support those attributes. Any proposed convention for tagging cache directories that depends on non-portable OS features would inherently have limited applicability and would not likely be widely adopted among portable applications. Applications are free to support system-specific extensions when available, but for maximum usefulness we really need one extremely simple, portable convention that caching applications can adopt with almost no effort, and that is the purpose of this proposal.

  • Why shouldn't all web browsers just use the same cache? Sharing a single Internet content cache among multiple web browsers or other web applications, all of which might be running concurrently, is a very useful and important goal - but it presents considerable technical challenges that are still being worked out. Even when such sharing is achieved for web content caches, however, many other applications maintain other types of on-disk caches in which they store entirely different kinds of data, such as intermediate results for long-running computations or swap space for large data sets. It is unreasonable to expect applications to share caches when those caches serve entirely different purposes and inherently must be managed differently. But it would still be useful, and it is much more realistic, to expect applications at least to make their caches easily identifiable as such by adopting a simple convention such as the one proposed here.

  • Why does the proposal only support cache directories - what if an application only has a single file that it uses as a cache? Because the tagging scheme is much simpler and easier to take advantage of this way, and imposes no great burden on caching applications. If you're not paranoid about checking cache directory tag file headers, then most existing, unmodified backup/archive applications can immediately take advantage of this tagging convention via some pretty trivial shell-fu. For example: 

    	find . -name CACHEDIR.TAG | sed -e 's/[/]CACHEDIR.TAG$//g' >/tmp/excludes
	tar cvf /tmp/backup.tar --exclude-from=/tmp/excludes .
    Trying to support the tagging of individual files within a directory would probably mean at least having to parse text files and scan them for file names, making the convention much more complex and potentially delicate at the outset. And when it comes to standards that affect what data gets backed up and what doesn't, we obviously would like them to be as simple and robust as possible.

    For those existing caching applications that keep all their cached data in a single file, it should not be inordinately difficult for them just to move that cache file down one level into its own separate subdirectory, and write a cache directory tag into that new subdirectory alongside the cache file. Such a slight directory structure change should not cause any backward compatibility problems, since by definition any cache file in the old location is regenerable and can just be deleted or moved. And users won't care because they aren't normally expected to see directly or understand the application's cache files or directories anyway.

blocks.html_en:
Why not just standardize the location where applications keep their cache directories? First, there are already multiple conflicting standards: e.g., the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard specifies /var/cache, KDE currently uses /var/tmp, and the XDG Base Directory Specification specifies the use of a user-customizable environment variable, falling back on $(HOME)/.cache in the user's home directory. Even if one such standard eventually "wins", there is inevitable tension between standardizing the placement of application caches and allowing users to customize it. The XDG specification clearly recognizes this tension and accounts for it, but its very flexibility makes it difficult or impossible for systemwide utilities to locate each user's cache directory reliably, even if all applications were to conform to the XDG standard.

  • Why not just use the existing "no-backup" or "no-dump" extended file attributes that some operating systems support? Because only some operating systems (and generally only certain specific file systems in them) support those attributes. Any proposed convention for tagging cache directories that depends on non-portable OS features would inherently have limited applicability and would not likely be widely adopted among portable applications. Applications are free to support system-specific extensions when available, but for maximum usefulness we really need one extremely simple, portable convention that caching applications can adopt with almost no effort, and that is the purpose of this proposal.

  • Why shouldn't all web browsers just use the same cache? Sharing a single Internet content cache among multiple web browsers or other web applications, all of which might be running concurrently, is a very useful and important goal - but it presents considerable technical challenges that are still being worked out. Even when such sharing is achieved for web content caches, however, many other applications maintain other types of on-disk caches in which they store entirely different kinds of data, such as intermediate results for long-running computations or swap space for large data sets. It is unreasonable to expect applications to share caches when those caches serve entirely different purposes and inherently must be managed differently. But it would still be useful, and it is much more realistic, to expect applications at least to make their caches easily identifiable as such by adopting a simple convention such as the one proposed here.

  • Why does the proposal only support cache directories - what if an application only has a single file that it uses as a cache? Because the tagging scheme is much simpler and easier to take advantage of this way, and imposes no great burden on caching applications. If you're not paranoid about checking cache directory tag file headers, then most existing, unmodified backup/archive applications can immediately take advantage of this tagging convention via some pretty trivial shell-fu. For example: 

    	find . -name CACHEDIR.TAG | sed -e 's/[/]CACHEDIR.TAG$//g' >/tmp/excludes
	tar cvf /tmp/backup.tar --exclude-from=/tmp/excludes .
    Trying to support the tagging of individual files within a directory would probably mean at least having to parse text files and scan them for file names, making the convention much more complex and potentially delicate at the outset. And when it comes to standards that affect what data gets backed up and what doesn't, we obviously would like them to be as simple and robust as possible.

    For those existing caching applications that keep all their cached data in a single file, it should not be inordinately difficult for them just to move that cache file down one level into its own separate subdirectory, and write a cache directory tag into that new subdirectory alongside the cache file. Such a slight directory structure change should not cause any backward compatibility problems, since by definition any cache file in the old location is regenerable and can just be deleted or moved. And users won't care because they aren't normally expected to see directly or understand the application's cache files or directories anyway.

sentences.en: And when it comes to standards that affect what data gets backed up and what doesn't, we obviously would like them to be as simple and robust as possible.

blocks.html_en:
• Why not just use the existing "no-backup" or "no-dump" extended file attributes that some operating systems support? Because only some operating systems (and generally only certain specific file systems in them) support those attributes. Any proposed convention for tagging cache directories that depends on non-portable OS features would inherently have limited applicability and would not likely be widely adopted among portable applications. Applications are free to support system-specific extensions when available, but for maximum usefulness we really need one extremely simple, portable convention that caching applications can adopt with almost no effort, and that is the purpose of this proposal.

  • Why shouldn't all web browsers just use the same cache? Sharing a single Internet content cache among multiple web browsers or other web applications, all of which might be running concurrently, is a very useful and important goal - but it presents considerable technical challenges that are still being worked out. Even when such sharing is achieved for web content caches, however, many other applications maintain other types of on-disk caches in which they store entirely different kinds of data, such as intermediate results for long-running computations or swap space for large data sets. It is unreasonable to expect applications to share caches when those caches serve entirely different purposes and inherently must be managed differently. But it would still be useful, and it is much more realistic, to expect applications at least to make their caches easily identifiable as such by adopting a simple convention such as the one proposed here.

  • Why does the proposal only support cache directories - what if an application only has a single file that it uses as a cache? Because the tagging scheme is much simpler and easier to take advantage of this way, and imposes no great burden on caching applications. If you're not paranoid about checking cache directory tag file headers, then most existing, unmodified backup/archive applications can immediately take advantage of this tagging convention via some pretty trivial shell-fu. For example: 

    	find . -name CACHEDIR.TAG | sed -e 's/[/]CACHEDIR.TAG$//g' >/tmp/excludes
	tar cvf /tmp/backup.tar --exclude-from=/tmp/excludes .
    Trying to support the tagging of individual files within a directory would probably mean at least having to parse text files and scan them for file names, making the convention much more complex and potentially delicate at the outset. And when it comes to standards that affect what data gets backed up and what doesn't, we obviously would like them to be as simple and robust as possible.

    For those existing caching applications that keep all their cached data in a single file, it should not be inordinately difficult for them just to move that cache file down one level into its own separate subdirectory, and write a cache directory tag into that new subdirectory alongside the cache file. Such a slight directory structure change should not cause any backward compatibility problems, since by definition any cache file in the old location is regenerable and can just be deleted or moved. And users won't care because they aren't normally expected to see directly or understand the application's cache files or directories anyway.

blocks.html_en:
Why shouldn't all web browsers just use the same cache? Sharing a single Internet content cache among multiple web browsers or other web applications, all of which might be running concurrently, is a very useful and important goal - but it presents considerable technical challenges that are still being worked out. Even when such sharing is achieved for web content caches, however, many other applications maintain other types of on-disk caches in which they store entirely different kinds of data, such as intermediate results for long-running computations or swap space for large data sets. It is unreasonable to expect applications to share caches when those caches serve entirely different purposes and inherently must be managed differently. But it would still be useful, and it is much more realistic, to expect applications at least to make their caches easily identifiable as such by adopting a simple convention such as the one proposed here.

  • Why does the proposal only support cache directories - what if an application only has a single file that it uses as a cache? Because the tagging scheme is much simpler and easier to take advantage of this way, and imposes no great burden on caching applications. If you're not paranoid about checking cache directory tag file headers, then most existing, unmodified backup/archive applications can immediately take advantage of this tagging convention via some pretty trivial shell-fu. For example: 

    	find . -name CACHEDIR.TAG | sed -e 's/[/]CACHEDIR.TAG$//g' >/tmp/excludes
	tar cvf /tmp/backup.tar --exclude-from=/tmp/excludes .
    Trying to support the tagging of individual files within a directory would probably mean at least having to parse text files and scan them for file names, making the convention much more complex and potentially delicate at the outset. And when it comes to standards that affect what data gets backed up and what doesn't, we obviously would like them to be as simple and robust as possible.

    For those existing caching applications that keep all their cached data in a single file, it should not be inordinately difficult for them just to move that cache file down one level into its own separate subdirectory, and write a cache directory tag into that new subdirectory alongside the cache file. Such a slight directory structure change should not cause any backward compatibility problems, since by definition any cache file in the old location is regenerable and can just be deleted or moved. And users won't care because they aren't normally expected to see directly or understand the application's cache files or directories anyway.

blocks.html_en: Conclusion

sentences.en: Conclusion
sentences.zh: 结论

blocks.html_en: By adopting the convention described above of writing cache directory tags where appropriate, applications that create and manage cache directories will make it much easier for users and system administrators to identify and manage "soft" storage areas whose contents are useful for performance but do not need to be backed up or synchronized across machines. This proposal deliberately attempts to adopt the simplest reasonable and portable solution, in the interest of maximizing robustness while minimizing cost of adoption, and hence hopefully maximizing its chance of eventual widespread adoption. The proposal nevertheless leaves room for future enhancements, in which applications might include within their cache directory tags additional useful information about the semantics and management of their cache directories.

sentences.en: By adopting the convention described above of writing cache directory tags where appropriate, applications that create and manage cache directories will make it much easier for users and system administrators to identify and manage "soft" storage areas whose contents are useful for performance but do not need to be backed up or synchronized across machines.
sentences.zh: 通过采用上述在适当的地方写入缓存目录标签的约定，创建和管理缓存目录的应用程序将使用户和系统管理员更容易识别和管理“软”存储区域，这些区域的内容对性能有用，但不需要跨机器备份或同步。

sentences.en: This proposal deliberately attempts to adopt the simplest reasonable and portable solution, in the interest of maximizing robustness while minimizing cost of adoption, and hence hopefully maximizing its chance of eventual widespread adoption.
sentences.zh: 该提案有意尝试采用最简单、合理和可移植的解决方案，以最大限度地提高鲁棒性，同时最大限度地降低采用成本，从而希望最大限度地提高其最终广泛采用的机会。

sentences.en: The proposal nevertheless leaves room for future enhancements, in which applications might include within their cache directory tags additional useful information about the semantics and management of their cache directories.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，该提案还是为未来的增强留下了空间，其中应用程序可能会在其缓存目录标记中包含有关其缓存目录的语义和管理的其他有用信息。

blocks.html_en: Changes

sentences.zh: 变化

blocks.html_en:
• Version 0.6, November 4, 2019: Web page consolidation and restructuring; no substantive changes.

  • Version 0.5, October 27, 2004: Added Security Considerations section in response to a potential vulnerability pointed out by Gregory P. Smith.

  • Version 0.4, August 10, 2004: Changed cache tag file name from ".IsCacheDirectory" to "CACHEDIR.TAG", to be compatible with old 14-character POSIX file systems and 8.3-character MS-DOS file systems.

  • Version 0.3, July 29, 2004: Recommend that apps include a comment in their tags referring back to this spec. Recommend that backup/archival apps verify tag headers. Discuss tag filename length issue.

  • Version 0.2, July 23, 2004: Fleshed out Alternative Approaches section based on various feedback. Removed reference to thumbnail managing standard since the thumbnails directory doesn't quite have pure cache semantics.

  • Version 0.1, July 19, 2004: First public release.

content_html_zh:
July 19, 2004

版本 0.6（更改）

由布莱恩·福特提议

抽象的

许多应用程序创建和管理目录，其中包含有关存储在其他位置的内容的缓存信息，例如缓存的 Web 内容或图像或电影的缩略图大小版本。 为了速度和存储效率，我们通常希望避免备份、归档或以其他方式不必要地复制此类目录，但在数据传输操作期间识别并单独排除每个此类目录是一件痛苦的事情。 我提出了一种极其简单的约定，应用程序可以通过该约定可靠地“标记”它们创建的任何缓存目录，以便备份系统和其他数据管理实用程序轻松识别。 然后，数据管理实用程序可以根据用户认为合适的情况留意或忽略这些标签。

问题

许多应用程序创建和管理某种类型的缓存目录：在其中存储临时信息的目录，这些信息的存储可以有利于应用程序的性能，但如果丢失，可以轻松地从其他“主”数据源重新生成。 例如，大多数 Web 浏览器都有一个缓存目录，用于存储下载的 Internet 内容（Mozilla 通常在 $(HOME)/.mozilla 下的某处使用名为 Cache 的目录）。 当备份、同步或将用户的主目录从一台计算机传输到另一台计算机时，我们通常希望完全避免保存或传输缓存目录，因为：

  • 缓存目录的内容通常没有长期存档价值，因为它仅反映存储在其他地方的源材料，并且通常以缩减或不完整的形式 - 例如，仅反映用户最近访问过的网页部分，或者用户浏览过的图片的缩略图大小版本。
  • 缓存目录可能会增长到相当大的大小 - 特别是 Web 浏览器的缓存目录 - 可能会浪费大量的备份存储空间和/或网络带宽。
  • 即使用户只是浏览或查看文件，缓存目录的内容也往往会频繁更改，这会混淆增量备份或同步软件，使其在目录树中看到许多频繁的“错误更改”，从而降低增量算法的效率。
  • 缓存目录中的文件通常命名非常神秘（例如，基于原始源材料的名称或内容的哈希）。 在遍历用户主目录的操作过程中，看到数千个此类无意义的文件名滚动过去，这充其量是令人烦恼的，并且可能会令没有经验的用户感到困惑或害怕。

然而，有很多应用程序使用缓存目录。 虽然一些不断发展的标准试图标准化此类目录的位置，但大多数应用程序在不可预测的位置创建自己的缓存目录，并独立于其他应用程序来管理它们。 因此，即使对于能够轻松识别缓存目录的经验丰富的用户来说，找出并列出用于备份或其他目录遍历操作的所有适当的 --exclude 选项也变得非常乏味。 某些应用程序（例如最新版本的 KDE）将其缓存目录保留在 /tmp 或 /var/tmp 中，这通过将缓存内容完全从用户的主目录中获取来至少部分解决了这个问题。 但是，应用程序或用户可能不希望将这些缓存放在 /tmp 或 /var/tmp 中：例如，这些目录可能位于空间有限的不同分区上，或者其中的文件可能被系统过于频繁地清除（例如，一天后或每次重新启动时），以允许缓存有效地达到其预期目的。

文件系统层次结构标准指定了一个系统范围的 /var/cache 目录，旨在保存此类缓存信息，但该目录通常不是全局可写的，因此只能由系统预打包或由系统管理员安装的应用程序使用。 XDG 基本目录规范建议应用程序在用户环境变量指定的位置创建缓存，如果该环境变量不存在，则在用户主目录的标准子目录中创建缓存。 XDG 约定允许应用程序在没有特殊权限的情况下运行，但它不能帮助系统范围的备份或数据管理实用程序可靠地定位各个用户的缓存目录，因为每个用户可能在其登录配置文件中设置不同的环境变量。

建议的解决方案

鉴于目前对于应用程序应在何处存储其缓存信息不存在任何完美的协议，我提出了一个非常简单的约定，至少允许有效地识别此类信息。 无论应用程序决定（或配置为）将其缓存目录放置在何处，它都应该在此目录中放置一个名为：

缓存目录标签

该文件必须是普通文件，例如不能是符号链接。 此外，该文件的前 43 个八位字节必须包含以下 ASCII 标头字符串：

签名：8a477f597d28d172789f06886806bc55

标头签名中大小写很重要，文件中“S”之前不能有空格或其他字符，并且冒号之后恰好有一个空格字符（ASCII 代码 32 十进制）。 文件中的标头字符串不必后跟 LF 或 CR/LF 才能将文件识别为有效的缓存目录标记。 签名中的十六进制值恰好是字符串“.IsCacheDirectory”的 MD5 哈希值。 需要此签名标头以避免名为 CACHEDIR.TAG 的不相关文件被数据管理实用程序错误地解释为缓存目录标记，并且（例如）导致不备份有价值的数据。

标记文件的其余部分的内容当前未指定，但它应该是 UTF-8 编码的纯文本文件，并且任何以哈希字符（“#”）开头的行都应被视为注释，并被读取该文件的任何软件忽略。

此后，我们将引用上面指定的名称的文件，并在其内容的开头具有所需的签名，作为缓存目录标记。

为了让任何偶然发现并直接查看缓存目录标记的人受益，建议应用程序在文件中包含引用此规范的注释。 例如：

Signature: 8a477f597d28d172789f06886806bc55 # 该文件是（应用程序名称）创建的缓存目录标记。 # 有关缓存目录标签的信息，请参阅： # http://www.brynosaurus.com/cachedir/

如果/当本提案成为正式标准时，本规范的官方“主页”URL 可能会在某个时候从上述内容发生变化，但在这种情况下，适当的转发链接将保留在旧位置。

应用语义

给定目录中缓存目录标签的存在表明该目录的全部内容（包括其下的任何和所有子目录）由缓存信息组成，如果需要，可以从位于其他位置的适当源材料重新生成这些信息。 创建和/或使用缓存目录的应用程序应将单个缓存目录标记写入其内容代表缓存信息的最顶层目录。 例如，如果 Mozilla 创建一个名为 $(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache 的目录来保存从 Internet 下载的缓存内容，那么它会在其中创建一个名为：$(HOME)/.mozilla/default/dg8t1ih0.slt/Cache/CACHEDIR.TAG 的缓存目录标记。 只需一个缓存目录标签即可标记缓存内容的整个子目录树。 如果缓存目录标签消失，应用程序还应该重新生成缓存目录标签：例如，如果缓存目录的全部内容被删除而目录本身没有被删除。

数据备份、传输或同步软件（如果配置为这样做）可以将目录中存在的缓存目录标记解释为从备份或传输操作中自动排除该目录（包括任何子目录）的命令。 我让此类应用程序的设计者来决定这种“自动排除”行为是否应该是默认行为，或者仅在用户明确要求时才采用。 短期内可能需要采取一些保守措施，但从长远来看，默认情况下自动排除缓存目录可以提高效率和易用性。

为了最大程度地防止意外数据丢失，强烈建议任何具有缓存目录标记内置支持的工具在断定该文件确实是缓存目录标记之前验证它找到的名为 CACHEDIR.TAG 的任何文件是否是常规文件并且实际上包含上面指定的 43 字节标头。

用户友好的文件管理器和其他目录层次结构浏览软件可能还希望注意到目录中缓存目录标签的存在，并通知用户该目录的内容是自动管理的，如果直接“浏览”则不太可能有多大意义。

系统管理软件（例如 du）可能提供一种方法来定位、汇总和报告分区上所有缓存目录的空间使用情况，帮助管理员确定文件系统上正在使用多少“软”空间，必要时可以轻松释放这些空间。 类似地，实用程序可能允许管理员清除系统上的所有缓存目录，或者删除缓存目录中超过特定​​“期限”的文件。

然而，系统软件不应该纯粹自动地定期检查并单方面删除任意缓存目录中的“旧”文件。 不同的应用程序需要能够以不同的方式管理其缓存；对于一个应用程序或系统来说被视为“旧”的缓存数据对于另一个应用程序或系统来说可能不会被视为“旧”（例如，如果某些数据需要很长时间才能重新生成）。 将来，该标准可能会扩展为允许应用程序在其缓存目录标记中写入附加元数据，从而为系统实用程序提供在全局基础上智能管理应用程序缓存所需的附加信息。 然而，目前引入缓存目录标签的主要目的仅仅是为了轻松避免不必要地将缓存数据从一台计算机备份或复制到另一台计算机。

安全考虑

在自动系统备份中“盲目”使用缓存目录标签可能会增加入侵者或恶意软件对系统造成的损害。 用户或系统管理员可能不太可能注意到恶意插入

CACHDIR.TAG

进入一个重要的目录，而不是彻底删除该目录，例如，导致该目录的内容从常规备份中被忽略。 为了减轻这种风险，备份软件至少应该告知用户哪些目录由于缓存目录标签的存在而被忽略。 自动增量备份软件可能会维护一个“批准的”缓存目录列表，每当出现新的缓存目录标签时，只有在获得系统管理员批准后才需要注意它们。 简而言之，为了在面临安全威胁时保持备份的稳健性，缓存目录标记只能被视为提示，而不能被视为应该备份和不应该备份的内容的“最终决定”。

替代方法

本节讨论在制定本提案时考虑的一些替代方案，以及按规定设计该提案的基本原理。 它采用迷你常见问题解答的形式，解决最常见的问题。

  • 为什么不直接标准化应用程序保存缓存目录的位置呢？ 首先，已经存在多个相互冲突的标准：例如，文件系统层次结构标准指定 /var/cache，KDE 当前使用 /var/tmp，而 XDG 基本目录规范指定使用用户可自定义的环境变量，并依赖于用户主目录中的 $(HOME)/.cache。 即使这样的一个标准最终“获胜”，标准化应用程序缓存的放置和允许用户自定义它之间也不可避免地存在紧张关系。 XDG 规范清楚地认识到这种紧张并对其进行了解释，但其灵活性使得系统范围的实用程序很难或不可能可靠地定位每个用户的缓存目录，即使所有应用程序都符合 XDG 标准。 为什么不直接使用某些操作系统支持的现有“无备份”或“无转储”扩展文件属性？ 因为只有某些操作系统（通常只有其中的某些特定文件系统）支持这些属性。 任何依赖于非便携式操作系统功能的标记缓存目录的提议约定本质上都具有有限的适用性，并且不太可能在便携式应用程序中广泛采用。 应用程序可以自由地支持系统特定的扩展（如果可用），但为了获得最大的实用性，我们确实需要一种极其简单、可移植的约定，缓存应用程序几乎可以毫不费力地采用该约定，这就是本提案的目的。 为什么所有网络浏览器不应该使用相同的缓存？ 在多个 Web 浏览器或其他 Web 应用程序（所有这些都可能同时运行）之间共享单个 Internet 内容缓存是一个非常有用且重要的目标，但它带来了相当大的技术挑战，这些挑战仍在解决中。 然而，即使 Web 内容缓存实现了这种共享，许多其他应用程序也会维护其他类型的磁盘缓存，在其中存储完全不同类型的数据，例如长时间运行计算的中间结果或大型数据集的交换空间。 当这些缓存服务于完全不同的目的并且本质上必须以不同的方式管理时，期望应用程序共享缓存是不合理的。 但期望应用程序至少通过采用一种简单的约定（例如此处提出的约定）使其缓存易于识别，仍然是有用的，而且更加现实。 为什么该提案仅支持缓存目录 - 如果应用程序只有一个文件用作缓存怎么办？ 因为这种方式的标记方案更简单、更容易利用，并且不会给缓存应用程序带来太大的负担。 如果您对检查缓存目录标记文件头并不偏执，那么大多数现有的、未修改的备份/归档应用程序可以通过一些相当简单的 shell-fu 立即利用此标记约定。 例如：查找 . -名称 CACHEDIR.TAG | sed -e 's/[/]CACHEDIR.TAG$//g' >/tmp/excludes tar cvf /tmp/backup.tar --exclude-from=/tmp/excludes 。 尝试支持目录中单个文件的标记可能意味着至少必须解析文本文件并扫描它们的文件名，这使得约定在一开始就变得更加复杂并且可能更加微妙。 当谈到影响哪些数据备份和哪些数据不备份的标准时，我们显然希望它们尽可能简单和强大。 对于那些将所有缓存数据保存在单个文件中的现有缓存应用程序来说，只需将该缓存文件向下移动一级到其自己的单独子目录中，并将缓存目录标记与缓存文件一起写入该新子目录中，应该不会太困难。 如此轻微的目录结构更改不应导致任何向后兼容性问题，因为根据定义，旧位置中的任何缓存文件都是可重新生成的，并且可以删除或移动。 用户不会关心，因为他们通常不会直接看到或理解应用程序的缓存文件或目录。

结论

通过采用上述在适当的地方写入缓存目录标签的约定，创建和管理缓存目录的应用程序将使用户和系统管理员更容易识别和管理“软”存储区域，这些区域的内容对性能有用，但不需要跨机器备份或同步。 该提案有意尝试采用最简单、合理和可移植的解决方案，以最大限度地提高鲁棒性，同时最大限度地降低采用成本，从而希望最大限度地提高其最终广泛采用的机会。 尽管如此，该提案还是为未来的增强留下了空间，其中应用程序可能会在其缓存目录标记中包含有关其缓存目录的语义和管理的其他有用信息。
